claim: The C-BARQ validated behavioral questionnaire compares an individual dog's scores against population averages from 50,000+ dogs — demonstrating that individual-level behavioral assessment requires population reference data, not breed stereotypes.
stance_target: individual temperament signals should outweigh breed priors in behavioral assessment
scope_conditions: C-BARQ database, University of Pennsylvania; 50,000 dogs, 300+ breeds; Hsu & Serpell 2003, operational since 2005
quoted_support: Because each dog's results are based on calculated population averages, the C-BARQ assessment provides a truly objective diagnosis of the relative severity of the dog's problems. Its scores are compared automatically with this very large reference sample of dogs using standard algorithms.

claim: Behavioral problems are the leading cause of premature death by euthanasia in companion dogs and the primary reason dogs are surrendered to shelters — establishing behavioral assessment at intake as a high-stakes rescue intervention.
stance_target: prioritization of behavioral intake assessment in rescue operations
scope_conditions: C-BARQ documentation citing broader veterinary behavioral medicine consensus; widely acknowledged in field
quoted_support: Behavior problems are widely acknowledged to be the most important cause of premature death (by euthanasia) in companion dogs, as well as being the primary reason why dogs are surrendered to animal shelters and rescue groups.

claim: The mini C-BARQ (Duffy, Kruger & Serpell, 2015) is specifically designed for shelter intake screening and provides more reliable behavioral data than in-house temperament tests or breed assessment alone.
stance_target: structured behavioral intake instruments over breed-based assumptions
scope_conditions: C-BARQ documentation; mini C-BARQ published 2015; shelter/adoption center context
quoted_support: The C-BARQ, and especially its short form (the mini C-BARQ; Duffy, Kruger & Serpell, 2015), provides shelters and adoption centers with a valuable tool for screening dogs on intake; and when used in combination with in-house temperament tests, can help to guide rational intervention and adoption strategies.
